text: From modest beginnings GROMACS has evolved over the last two decades into one of the most widely used biomolecular simulation programs in the world. What began as a fast program to simulate small biological systems has developed into a flexible, heterogeneous program capable of simulations on some of the worlds largest supercomputers. This article describes the strategies that have been employed and the tactics that have been employed to achieve high performance, an absolute that is needed at all levels to enable a simulation to reach its full potential. In this paper we describe the new features of GROMACS 4.6 including SIMD support on all modern architectures, GPU acceleration, as well as new hybrid OpenMP/ MPI multi-level parallelism that distributes the workload between different levels of the simulation within a node as well as between different nodes. In order to support the new hardware in the simulations some of the basic neighbor-searching algorithms had to be almost rewritten from scratch. In this article we highlight the challenge of achieving exascale performance by the necessity of having very fine grain parallelism in such simulations and also describe the many software development tools and procedures that have been implemented to develop a simulation code of such complexity.
